categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From monarchy to republic to empire — five regimes in 26 years

entries.label: Louis XVI — from king to prisoner
entries.hint: Louis XVI moves from Versailles to Paris under popular pressure in October 1789 — his flight to Varennes in June 1791 destroys his credibility; the storming of the Tuileries (10 August 1792) ends the monarchy; he is executed on 21 January 1793.
entries.note: Louis XVI's diary entry for 14 Jul 1789: 'Rien' — Archives nationales KK 622. The flight to Varennes 20–21 Jun 1791: documented in eyewitness accounts and official records. The trial records: Archives nationales, série W 293. The execution 21 Jan 1793: Moniteur Universel 22 Jan 1793 (BnF). Jordan (1979), The King's Trial: the standard study of Louis XVI's trial.

entries.label: The Republic — from the Convention to the Directory
entries.hint: The First Republic passes through the Terror (1793–1794), Thermidor, and the Directory — five years of political violence, dechristianisation, revolutionary war and corruption; the Revolutionary archive is one of the richest primary source collections in European history.
entries.note: Archives parlementaires (1789–1860): the verbatim record of the Revolutionary assemblies — digitised by Stanford/BnF (French Revolution Digital Archive). The Moniteur Universel (official newspaper of the Revolution): BnF Gallica. The Terror's records: Archives nationales, Tribunal Révolutionnaire série W. Schama (1989), Citizens: A Chronicle of the French Revolution: the standard narrative history.

entries.label: Napoleon Bonaparte — from First Consul to Emperor
entries.hint: Napoleon seizes power in the coup of 18 Brumaire — rules as First Consul (1799–1804) then Emperor; his Civil Code (1804), prefectural system and Concordat with the Church reshape France permanently; defeated at Waterloo on 18 June 1815 he abdicates a second time.
entries.note: Napoleon's correspondence: published in 32 volumes (1858–1870) by the Commission Impériale; over 20,000 letters. Las Cases, Mémorial de Sainte-Hélène (1823): Napoleon's own retrospective account — BnF. The Civil Code (Code Napoléon): Bulletin des Lois 1804. Waterloo: 18 Jun 1815 confirmed in military records on both sides. Tulard (1977), Napoléon ou le mythe du sauveur: the standard French biography.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Revolutionary wars, the Haitian Revolution and the Napoleonic wars

entries.label: Valmy — the Revolution defends itself
entries.hint: The revolutionary army stops the Prussian-Austrian invasion at Valmy — a cannonade that barely touches either side; but the Prussians retreat; Goethe (present as an observer) remarks 'From this place and from this day forth commences a new era in the world's history'.
entries.note: Kellermann's dispatches: Archives de la Guerre A1. Goethe's account: Campagne in Frankreich (1822) — he was an eyewitness. The battle's minor tactical significance vs enormous symbolic importance: documented in all contemporary accounts. Moniteur Universel 24 Sep 1792: BnF. Forrest (2011), Napoleon's Men: analyses the Revolutionary army's social composition.

entries.label: The Terror — 16,594 official executions
entries.loc: Paris (guillotine at Place de la Révolution); provinces (noyades)
entries.hint: The Committee of Public Safety under Robespierre executes 16,594 people by guillotine (official figure) and perhaps 40,000 in total including mass drownings (noyades) in Nantes and shootings in Lyon; the Tribunal Révolutionnaire records survive as a primary source of extraordinary completeness.
entries.note: Tribunal Révolutionnaire records: Archives nationales, série W — the most complete record of any mass political execution in European history. The 16,594 official figure: Greer (1935), The Incidence of the Terror. Noyades in Nantes: documented in Carrier's dispatches (Archives nationales) and local records. Linton (2013), Choosing Terror: uses the tribunal records. Robespierre's fall 9 Thermidor (27 Jul 1794): Convention records (Archives parlementaires).

entries.label: Haitian Revolution — enslaved people overthrow slavery
entries.hint: The enslaved population of Saint-Domingue rises on 22 August 1791 — the only successful slave revolution in history; after 13 years of war against France, Spain and Britain, Haiti declares independence on 1 January 1804; Napoleon sends an expedition that fails disastrously.
entries.note: The night of 22 Aug 1791: documented in colonial records (ANOM) and contemporary white colonist accounts. The Declaration of Independence 1 Jan 1804: original document (Haitian National Archives). Napoleon's expedition: Archives de la Guerre A1 and Archives nationales, Colonies CC9. Dubois (2004), Avengers of the New World: the standard modern history using French, Haitian and British archives.

entries.label: Austerlitz — Napoleon's greatest victory
entries.loc: Austerlitz (mod. Slavkov u Brna, Czech Republic)
entries.hint: Napoleon defeats the combined Austrian-Russian army in less than a day at Austerlitz — his most brilliant tactical victory; he dictates the subsequent peace terms from the battlefield; the Emperor's 18th Bulletin (3 Dec 1805) is his own account.
entries.note: Napoleon's Bulletins de la Grande Armée (official campaign bulletins): Archives nationales, AF IV. Austrian military records: Kriegsarchiv, Vienna. Russian military records: RGVIA, Moscow. Austrian-Russian casualties: c.36,000 vs French c.9,000 — documented in after-action reports. Chandler (1966), The Campaigns of Napoleon: the standard modern tactical study.

entries.label: Waterloo — the final defeat
entries.hint: Napoleon's final battle — Wellington and Blücher defeat him in 8 hours; 47,000 casualties in one day; Napoleon abdicates four days later; the battle is the most documented military engagement of the 19th century, with eyewitness accounts from hundreds of participants.
entries.note: Wellington's dispatches: British National Archives, WO 1/337 (his Waterloo despatch). Napoleon's abdication 22 Jun 1815: Archives nationales. Victor Hugo's Les Misérables (1862), the Waterloo chapter: a literary reconstruction but based on direct testimony from veterans. Roberts (2005), Waterloo: Napoleon's Last Gamble: uses archives on both sides. The battlefield: extant (UNESCO Memory of the World for the associated records).

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: The Declaration of Rights, the Civil Code and the new legal order

entries.label: Declaration of the Rights of Man — 26 August 1789
entries.hint: The National Assembly adopts the Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen on 26 August 1789 — 17 articles establishing natural rights, national sovereignty and the rule of law; it is the foundational document of modern human rights and directly influenced the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948).
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Original text: Archives nationales (AE/I/12). The vote on 26 Aug 1789: Archives parlementaires Vol.VIII (digitised). The Declaration's influence on the UDHR (1948): documented in the UN drafting committee records. Hunt (1996), The French Revolution and Human Rights: documentary anthology. The French Revolution Digital Archive (Stanford/BnF): digitised primary sources.

entries.label: Code Napoléon — the Civil Code of 1804
entries.hint: The Code Civil (Code Napoléon) is promulgated on 21 March 1804 — 2,281 articles codifying civil law, property rights, contracts and family law; it remains the basis of French law and has been adopted or influenced law codes in 70+ countries.
entries.note: Code Civil des Français: Bulletin des Lois, No.3909, 21 Mar 1804. Napoleon's personal role in the drafting sessions: documented in the Conseil d'État procès-verbaux (Archives nationales). Halpérin (1992), L'Impossible Code Civil: the standard study of its drafting. Current use in 70+ countries: documented by the Conseil constitutionnel's comparative law studies.

entries.label: Concordat of 1801 — Napoleon re-establishes the Church
entries.hint: Napoleon signs a Concordat with Pope Pius VII re-establishing the Catholic Church in France — it heals the Revolutionary schism, the Church accepts the loss of its confiscated property; still in force in Alsace-Moselle today.
entries.note: Concordat text 15 Jul 1801: Archives nationales (AF IV 1851). The negotiation: Consalvi's memoirs (Vatican Archives). Still in force in Alsace-Lorraine (returned from Germany after 1918): documented in French law. Ellis (2000), Napoleon: the standard English biography, ch.6 on the Concordat's strategic purpose.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Dechristianisation, Cult of the Supreme Being and the religious settlement

entries.label: Dechristianisation — Reason replaces God
entries.loc: Paris (Notre-Dame becomes Temple of Reason); provinces
entries.hint: The Terror's dechristianisation campaign converts churches to Temples of Reason, abolishes Sunday, renames months, and executes priests — Notre-Dame de Paris becomes a Temple of Reason on 10 November 1793; Robespierre then institutes the Cult of the Supreme Being.
entries.note: The ceremony at Notre-Dame 10 Nov 1793 (20 Brumaire Year II): Moniteur Universel 12 Nov 1793 (BnF). The dechristianisation decrees: Archives parlementaires. The Revolutionary calendar (introduced 22 Sep 1792): Bulletin des Lois. Vovelle (1988), The Revolution Against the Church: the standard study. The Cult of the Supreme Being festival 8 Jun 1794: Moniteur Universel.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: David, Géricault, the Revolutionary visual programme and Napoleon's cultural policy

entries.label: Jacques-Louis David — painter of the Revolution and Empire
entries.hint: David paints The Death of Marat (1793) — the Revolution's most powerful image; becomes Napoleon's official painter (The Coronation of Napoleon, 1807); his career from the Ancien Régime through Revolution to Empire is the most complete artistic document of the entire period.
entries.note: Death of Marat (1793): Musées Royaux des Beaux-Arts, Brussels. The Coronation of Napoleon (1807): Louvre (INV 1270). David's revolutionary activities: Archives nationales, série F17. His official commission records: Archives nationales, série O2 (Maison de l'Empéreur). Johnson (1993), Jacques-Louis David: Art in Metamorphosis: the standard study.

entries.label: Arc de Triomphe and the Napoleonic building programme
entries.hint: Napoleon orders the Arc de Triomphe on 2 December 1806 (first anniversary of Austerlitz) — 50 metres high, the largest triumphal arch in the world; completed under Louis-Philippe; it lists 660 generals' names and 128 battles; the building programme transforms Paris.
entries.note: Commission decree 2 Dec 1806: Archives nationales AF IV. Architect Chalgrin's plans: Archives nationales, Cartes et Plans. The arch's completion 1836: documented in construction records. The list of battles and generals: extant on the arch. Tulard (1985), Paris sous Louis XIV: contextualises the Napoleonic building programme.

entries.label: Beethoven's Eroica — Napoleon as muse and disappointment
entries.hint: Beethoven composes his Third Symphony dedicated to Napoleon — then, on hearing Napoleon had declared himself Emperor, tears off the dedication page and re-titles it simply 'Heroic Symphony'; the torn page survives, documenting the Enlightenment's disillusionment with Napoleon.
entries.note: The torn dedication page (autograph score): Gesellschaft der Musikfreunde, Vienna — the most famous crossed-out dedication in music history. Beethoven's letter about Napoleon to his pupil Ries: documented in Schindler (1840), Biographie von Ludwig van Beethoven. First performance (private) 9 Jun 1804, Vienna: documented. Cooper (2000), Beethoven: the standard biography.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Revolutionary ideology, Napoleon's propaganda and counter-revolutionary thought

entries.label: What Is the Third Estate? — Sieyès launches the Revolution
entries.hint: Abbé Sieyès's pamphlet Qu'est-ce que le tiers état? (January 1789) asks and answers its own question: 'Everything. What has it been until now in the political order? Nothing. What does it want? To become something' — the founding manifesto of the Revolution.
entries.note: Qu'est-ce que le tiers état?: published January 1789, Paris; BnF. 30,000 copies sold within weeks: documented in publisher's records. Sieyès's papers: Archives nationales, 284AP. Forsyth (1987), Reason and Revolution: The Political Thought of the Abbé Sieyès: the standard study.

entries.label: The Bulletins de la Grande Armée — Napoleon's self-narration
entries.hint: Napoleon writes or dictates 29 Bulletins de la Grande Armée — official campaign reports, published in the Moniteur, combining genuine military information with propaganda; the 29th Bulletin (1812) contains the famous line about the Russian campaign: 'The Emperor's health has never been better'.
entries.note: Bulletins: Archives nationales AF IV; published in Moniteur Universel. The 29th Bulletin (3 Dec 1812): Moniteur 17 Dec 1812 (BnF) — 'La santé de Sa Majesté n'a jamais été meilleure.' As primary sources: the Bulletins' reliability must be assessed against other records (they systematically undercount French losses). Bret (2004), Napoleon's Bulletins: the primary analytical study.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Revolutionary economy, assignats and the Continental System

entries.label: The assignats — revolutionary paper money and hyperinflation
entries.hint: The National Assembly issues assignats (paper currency backed by confiscated Church property) — by 1796 they have lost 97% of their face value through overprinting; the hyperinflation is the most dramatic monetary collapse in pre-modern European history.
entries.note: Assignat issue decrees: Archives parlementaires. Seymour Harris (1930), The Assignats: the standard economic study, with tables documenting the devaluation. The surviving assignat collection: BnF (Cabinet des médailles). The total face value issued: documented in Treasury records (Archives nationales, série Q). The connection between assignat collapse and the end of the Terror: argued by Schama (1989).

entries.label: Continental System — economic war against Britain
entries.hint: Napoleon's Berlin Decree (21 November 1806) closes all French-controlled ports to British goods — an attempted total economic blockade; it damages British trade but destroys French commerce too; Portugal's refusal to join triggers the Peninsular War; Russia's breach helps cause the 1812 invasion.
entries.note: Berlin Decree 21 Nov 1806: Archives nationales AF IV; Moniteur Universel 22 Nov 1806 (BnF). The system's economic effects: Crouzet (1964), Wars, Blockade and Economic Change in Europe 1792–1815, JEH 24: the standard economic analysis. Portugal's refusal: AGS Estado (the connection to the Peninsular War folio). The Russian breach 1810: documented in diplomatic archives.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Revolutionary ideas across Europe and Napoleon's imperial cultural policy

entries.label: The Revolution exports itself — European resonance
entries.loc: Europe — Britain; German states; Poland; Saint-Domingue
entries.hint: The French Revolution immediately resonates across Europe — Paine writes Rights of Man (1791), Wollstonecraft writes A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792), Kościuszko leads a Polish uprising (1794) citing the Declaration; the revolutionary ideas travel through pamphlets, newspapers and refugees.
entries.note: Thomas Paine, Rights of Man (1791–1792): British Library. Wollstonecraft, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792): BL. Kościuszko's proclamation (24 Mar 1794): citing the French Declaration — Polish State Archives. The network of Jacobin clubs outside France: documented in Archives nationales, série F7. Kates (1998), The French Revolution: Recent Debates and New Controversies.

entries.label: Napoleon's looting of European art — the Louvre as world museum
entries.hint: Napoleon's armies systematically remove art from conquered territories — 506 works from Italy (1796–1797), Egyptian antiquities, hundreds of paintings from Spain; the Louvre becomes the world's first universal museum; most works are returned after Waterloo but many remain.
entries.note: The Italian armistice terms (1796) demanding art works: Archives des Affaires Étrangères. The inventory of works seized: Archives des musées nationaux (Louvre). The 1815 return negotiations: Archives des Affaires Étrangères. McClellan (1994), Inventing the Louvre: the standard study of the museum's Revolutionary origins. The works not returned: documented in Louvre inventories.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: France reshapes the world — and is reshaped by it

entries.label: Napoleon reshapes the map of Europe
entries.hint: At its peak in 1810–1811, Napoleon's empire directly controls 70 million people across Europe — Holy Roman Empire dissolved (1806), Confederation of the Rhine, Kingdom of Westphalia; the map of Europe is redrawn more dramatically than at any point since the Roman Empire.
entries.note: The dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire 6 Aug 1806: the Emperor's renunciation document — Haus-, Hof- und Staatsarchiv, Vienna. The map of Napoleonic Europe: documented in the treaties series (Archives des Affaires Étrangères). Broers (2002), Europe Under Napoleon: the standard study of the empire's administration.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Olympe de Gouges — Declaration of the Rights of Woman
entries.hint: Olympe de Gouges publishes the Déclaration des droits de la femme et de la citoyenne in September 1791 — mirroring the 1789 Declaration article by article to argue women have the same rights as men; she is guillotined in November 1793; her text is the founding document of modern feminism.
entries.note: Déclaration des droits de la femme et de la citoyenne (Sep 1791): BnF. Her trial records: Archives nationales, Tribunal Révolutionnaire série W. Executed 3 Nov 1793: Moniteur Universel 4 Nov 1793 (BnF). Blanc (2003), Olympe de Gouges: the standard biography. The Declaration's text: explicitly parallels the 1789 Declaration — 'Article I: Woman is born free and lives equal to man in her rights.'

entries.label: Penal Code 1791 — homosexuality decriminalised in France
entries.hint: The Revolutionary Penal Code of 1791 removes sodomy from the list of crimes — France becomes the first major European country to decriminalise same-sex acts; the Napoleonic Code (1810) maintains this decriminalisation; the CATEGORICAL SHIFT is the logical extension of 'crimes without victims' Enlightenment philosophy.
entries.note: Penal Code 1791: Archives parlementaires, Vol.XXVI. The Napoleonic Penal Code 1810: Bulletin des Lois — maintains the 1791 decriminalisation. Crompton (1985), Byron and Greek Love, ch.1: analyses the 1791 decriminalisation. Sibalis (1996), 'The Regulation of Male Homosexuality in Revolutionary and Napoleonic France' in Merrick & Ragan (eds.): the primary scholarly study. France maintained decriminalisation throughout the 19th–20th century until today.

entries.label: Haitian Independence — the enslaved overthrow slavery
entries.hint: Haiti's declaration of independence on 1 January 1804 is the only successful revolution by enslaved people in history — the 500,000 enslaved Africans documented in the previous folio have abolished slavery and defeated Napoleon's army; the original Declaration of Independence is the primary document.
entries.note: Declaration of Haitian Independence 1 Jan 1804: original document discovered 2010 (University of Puerto Rico library collection, documented in Dubois & Garrigus 2006 appendix; confirmed authentic by Haitian National Archives). Napoleon's expedition records: Archives nationales, Colonies CC9. Dubois (2004), Avengers of the New World: uses French, Haitian and British archives. This is the most consequential CATEGORICAL SHIFT in the entire Lives corpus: enslaved people abolish their own legal category.

events.l: Sieyès: What Is the Third Estate?
Olympe de Gouges — Rights of Woman
